data.title: Lennox: Attractive Valuations And Impending Recovery In 2026 Make It A Good Buy
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I last covered Lennox International (NYSE:LII) in April with a Buy rating. The stock did well into midsummer but has since pulled back. That correction has mostly been tied to tough industry conditions - namely the drag from R410A inventory destocking and the canister shortage tied to the new R454B refrigerant. Those factors pressured sales and weighed on sentiment across residential HVAC names.

I see those as temporary headwinds. Looking out to 2026 and beyond, the setup looks much better. Sales should benefit from an end-market recovery as the rate cycle turns more supportive. The Fed has already started cutting, which over time should ease mortgage pressure and help both residential and light commercial demand.

On top of the macro, Lennox is executing well. The company has strengthened its distribution footprint, improved service levels, and is now leaning into emergency replacement demand thanks to added capacity at its New Mexico facility. The balance sheet is in good shape, with net leverage at just 1.0x EBITDA, leaving room for bolt-on M&A to complement organic growth.

Margins are another bright spot. Despite volume pressure, Lennox has been able to expand profitability through pricing, mix, and cost discipline. As volumes recover, operating leverage should provide another tailwind, alongside ongoing productivity and supply chain initiatives.

All in, I expect growth to re-accelerate in 2026 and beyond. With the stock trading below its historical P/E multiple, I think there's room for a re-rate as fundamentals improve. Between cyclical recovery, solid execution, and balance sheet flexibility, the risk/reward looks attractive. I'm reiterating my Buy rating.

Revenue Analysis And Outlook

Lennox reported about 3% revenue growth last quarter, with sales coming in at $1.50B. The top line was driven almost entirely by pricing and mix, which added roughly 11% and more than offset an 8% volume decline.

By segment, Home Comfort Solutions revenue was up 3% year-over-year. Volumes were down about 9%, pressured by both soft end-market demand and the drag from R410A destocking, with some added impact from R454B canister shortages. Price and mix were strong, though, up 12%, which more than carried the segment.

Building Climate Solutions grew nearly 5%. Volumes dipped 3% given broader market weakness, but emergency replacement activity provided a partial offset, and pricing/mix was again positive at 8%.

Overall, the quarter reflected resilient pricing power and strong execution, even as volume trends remained tough.

Moving forward, in the near term, Lennox is still dealing with some volume headwinds across its core residential and light commercial markets. While rates have started reversing, we are in a slow new construction season, and it will take a couple of quarters till next spring season for the sales to see a good pickup. On top of that, the refrigerant transition is creating a bit of noise. Distributors are focused on clearing out R410A systems before the year-end deadline, which means less pull-through on the new R454B units. That destocking dynamic probably sticks around through the rest of 2025.

Once we get past this transition, though, the setup looks much cleaner. Starting in 2026, the channel should be free of R410A inventory, which should help factory shipments and sell-through line up better. By then, we'll also be further along in the interest rate cycle, so both new and existing home sales should start to pick back up. The housing backdrop is still fundamentally solid, with a lot of pent-up demand sitting on the sidelines, and I'd expect the next cooling season to show a healthier demand.

On top of the macro, Lennox has some company-specific drivers that should help. The new factory at Saltillo, Mexico, has given it the capacity to go after the emergency replacement market in a way it really couldn't before. With better fulfillment and next-day delivery, the sales team is leaning into that channel, and it feels like a solid three- to five-year growth leg. Distribution is another piece. Between recent bolt-ons like the HVAC division of NSI's DuroDyne, and Supco, and ongoing investments to boost service levels, Lennox is strengthening its contractor reach and creating a real advantage versus peers. And with net leverage sitting at just 1.0x EBITDA, the balance sheet gives them room for more bolt-on M&A while still investing in the core.

The longer-term story is also tied to energy efficiency. Power costs keep moving up, data centers are adding to grid demand, and regulations keep pushing toward lower emissions. All of that plays into more efficient HVAC systems. We have recently seen a transition to R454B refrigerant from R410a, which helped the company's price/mix, and I am expecting more such transitions moving forward.

Put it all together, and while the next couple of quarters might still feel bumpy, the path into 2026 looks like a clean re-acceleration story.

Margin Analysis And Outlook

Margins came in strong in Q2, with Lennox posting a 170 bps year-over-year improvement and segment profit margin reaching 23.6%. The lift was driven by positive price/mix and solid execution on cost controls.

Home Comfort Solutions was the standout, with return on sales expanding 200 bps to 25.3%. That reflected strong pricing gains alongside good operational discipline. Building Climate Solutions also moved higher, up 60 bps year-over-year to 24.9%, supported by pricing and factory productivity improvements that offset inflationary headwinds.

Overall, the quarter showed that even with volumes under pressure, Lennox is managing to protect and even expand profitability through a combination of pricing power and efficiency.

I'm optimistic about Lennox's margin setup over the medium term. As volumes start to recover with the housing cycle and the R410A inventory clears, the company should get solid operating leverage. Higher throughput tends to flow through at pretty healthy increments, and with rate cuts starting to take hold, demand in 2026 looks like a good setup.

On costs, management has been running a tight playbook. They're targeting about $75 million in annual savings through their operational excellence program, and we're already seeing steady factory productivity gains. Lean manufacturing and Six Sigma are now embedded across the footprint, with projects around capacity optimization and automation helping push throughput higher while pulling labor cost per unit lower. Utilization at the Mexico facility should keep improving too, which adds another layer of margin support.

Tariffs and material inflation are still in the mix near term, but Lennox is working on the offsets. Strategic sourcing, supplier switches, and contract resets are all helping, and the company has enough pricing power and favorable mix from new R454B and higher-efficiency products to cover the rest.

Put it all together, margins should keep trending higher into 2026 and beyond. Volume recovery brings leverage, efficiency programs add structure, and mix shifts toward premium products provide incremental lift.

Valuation And Rating

Lennox is trading at 23.18x FY25 consensus EPS of $23.58 and 21.11x FY26 consensus EPS of $25.89. Over the past five years, the stock has averaged about 23.5x forward earnings. Since we are already approaching the end of this fiscal year, I believe it makes sense to look at next year's numbers, and on FY26 earnings, the stock is available at an attractive discount.

I think there's good potential for the growth to pick up and valuation multiple to re-rate as temporary headwinds roll off. Issues like refrigerant canister shortages, R410A destocking, and the drag from high mortgage rates are set to fade, and the rate cycle should turn more supportive into 2026. In addition, the company is executing well, as is evident from the margin improvement we saw last quarter.

If the stock were to revert to its five-year average multiple of 23.5x on FY26 consensus EPS of $25.89, it would imply a fair value of about $608 per share. That represents over 10% upside from the current price of ~$550. In addition, investors also get a ~1% dividend yield.

All in, with end markets improving, margins trending higher, and a solid balance sheet that supports both reinvestment and bolt-on M&A, I see the risk/reward as favorable. I'm maintaining a Buy rating on the stock.

Risks

I'm expecting lower rates to help unlock demand, especially in housing and replacement markets, but if the Fed cuts more slowly than expected or mortgage rates stay elevated longer, the recovery could be weaker than I'm building in. That would mean softer volumes and a slower revenue rebound, which would weigh on the stock.

Another risk is around M&A. Lennox has a good track record with bolt-on deals, but inorganic growth always carries extra uncertainty compared to organic execution. There's the possibility of overpaying for an asset, failing to realize synergies, or running into integration issues. Any of those could dilute returns and weigh on sentiment.

data.title: FEPI: Rare Moment When 25%+ Yield Is Suitable For Durable Income Investors
data.description:
The REX FANG & Innovation Equity Premium Income ETF (NASDAQ:FEPI) has been one of very few 20%+ yielding picks for which I have issued bullish (buy) articles. My previous piece on FEPI was circulated in March 2025 with a title: FEPI: This ~30% Yield Is Less Risky Than You Might Think.

The overarching thesis boiled down to actually solid and sustainable fundamentals (i.e., the way in which the yield is generated) despite optically unsustainable yield or what is typically associated with pure value traps.

Since my previous article, FEPI has delivered total returns of ~23%. It is worth noting that price terms, FEPI is up by ~5%, which is not a common characteristic for this high-yielding covered call ETF. Think of some YieldMax products that offer very lucrative current income streams - e.g., the YieldMax Ultra Option Income Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:ULTY) and YieldMax MSTR Option Income Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:MSTY). Their price performance figures on a YTD basis look like this:

In other words, for the likes of ULTY and MSTY, there is both significant NAV erosion and huge dependency on either flat or slightly upward/downward trending markets in order to avoid permanent value destruction. Since the market had recovered from the April tariff shock, both of these names are in a positive territory in terms of YTD total returns. Had there been no recovery and further downside, then most probably these names would be in red.

If we speak about FEPI and its current yield of ~25%, then I would say that the equity and dividend story has become stronger than before.

Let me explain.

Thesis review

The reason why FEPI's case has strengthened is primarily market-driven. What I mean by that is that the macro-level factors have recently formed in a specific manner that renders FEPI's value proposition more compelling than before.

Here is the thing.

The overall equity markets have continued to appreciate despite the clear build-up of risks in the system. Be it a weaker labor market, stubbornly high inflation, increased uncertainty on the geopolitical front, or basically any other structural macro variable, the overall direction of travel there has become less supportive for corporate profit growth. Granted, there are pockets of opportunity, especially in short-term interest rate-sensitive segments that benefit from lower base rates, but, all in all, I simply do not see why the S&P 500 (SPY) and/or the Nasdaq-100 (QQQ) should be registering one record after another.

With this assumption in mind, I also understand the rationale of still being long these richly priced areas, especially the QQQ segment. Such thinking of tech equities carrying valuation multiples that are too extended has been there basically since the moment the Fed embarked on an ultra-accommodative interest rate journey (post GFC). In other words, avoiding QQQ-like exposure might very likely come with material opportunity costs.

And this is where FEPI comes in.

In the table below, we can see almost the entire underlying holding base of FEPI, which is clearly skewed towards the large-cap tech players:

There are, of course, differences between FEPI's allocations (mostly weights) and the key exposure composition of QQQ, but the essence is very similar.

The reason we see such a Top 15 holdings list for FEPI is related to management's strategy to focus on two things: 1) large-cap, liquid tech names and 2) securities, which exhibit elevated implied volatility levels in order to maximize the option premium amount.

Yet, while FEPI carries direct investments in these 15 underlying names, the embedded risk and return dynamics are different. Because of the covered call overlay, the upside is capped, which is what allows FEPI to receive compensation for that in the form of relatively juicy option premiums.

This kind of strategy works well in the following scenarios:

  • The basket of underlying names is trading sideways.
  • The movements are slightly positive.
  • The movements are slightly negative.

As long as there is no significant and sudden upswing, the opportunity cost is almost non-existent. If, however, the 15 securities that are reflected in the table above suddenly plunge and remain depressed for a prolonged period of time, then obviously FEPI would suffer. But in such a case, the collected option premiums would kick in, providing some offset to the realized downside.

Here, one might theoretically point to a potential risk of FEPI recording deep dividend cuts under the previously mentioned downside scenario. This is something that has been evident among almost all YieldMax products (i.e., covered call ETFs that also offer juicy yields).

Let's take a look at FEPI's dividend chart:

In the first 4-5 months of 2025, when the markets were going through some material volatility events, FEPI did indeed suffer on the dividend front. We can see how the monthly dividend had declined from (on average) ~$1.05 per share in 2024 to ~$0.90 per share in early 2025. It makes sense because in covered call ETF products the relationship is as follows: the smaller the underlying asset base, the lower the option premium amounts. However, there are two important takeaways to consider:

  • After the realized bottom, FEPI did quickly assume a recovery path by announcing higher and higher monthly distributions (mostly because of the elevated volatility levels, which is another huge driver for option premium amounts).
  • Even with the 10% to 15% temporary dividend reduction, the offered yield remained extremely attractive (still in +25% territory).

The bottom line

In my opinion, FEPI is a rare exception that provides a very rich yield without utilizing an aggressive or overly speculative strategy. The reason why FEPI is able to offer ~25%+ yield lies in the concentrated holdings list in inherently volatile tech names. And, as we know, the higher the volatility, the higher the option premiums.

Having said that, we have to be cognizant of the risks that could stem from a potential correction in the QQQ names (and thus FEPI's holdings). I think that such a probability is material given the weakening economy and richly priced assets. At the same time, we (value tilted income investors) have to be honest to ourselves and appreciate that such thinking has for many of us resulted in significant opportunity costs.

So, as outlined above, I think that FEPI strikes the right balance between these two perspectives.

data.title: Axon Will Continue To Thrive Despite Valuation Concerns
data.description:
Axon Enterprise (NASDAQ:AXON) has accelerated spending on its cash acquisitions and strategic expansions in the last two years. To keep up with emerging trends such as AI, enterprise solutions, and drones, management is looking at opportunities both in the domestic and international markets. All this has put the company on a remarkable growth run, not just in terms of its valuation multiples but, more importantly, in terms of its business fundamentals. With already strong revenue visibility and attractive margins, the business is looking into more high-margin segments.

Alongside its TASER brand, which is a legacy umbrella of products, AXON also has a market-leading presence in the $7.5 billion global body camera segment. AXON could leverage its strong foothold within this space, which will experience a growth of high mid-teens, compounding into a circa $25 billion market over the next decade.

The stock has delivered a 21% return since the start of the year, outperforming the broader S&P 500 (14%) and Nasdaq (19%). However, compared to many other peers within the A&D universe, it has still lagged behind. Despite poor valuation grading as per Seeking Alpha, I am optimistic about the future prospects, and will present my bullish thesis on AXON.

Products and Services

Axon started as a Taser manufacturer nearly three decades ago. The company pivoted to software as a service (SaaS) during the early 2010s, beginning primarily with the focus on Evidence.com, a cloud-based evidence management platform that the company used to manage the videos from its body-cams. Today, it is successfully integrating AI and virtual reality in its product suite, another pivot that is a testament to the company’s innovative business approach. It is the hardware stack that forms the basis of the company’s ecosystem. It builds on the company’s trademark TASER devices that help public safety officials’ training using virtual reality solutions.

The tasers are well complemented by the body cameras and in-car cameras and sensors that not only ensure better operations but also add to transparency. Then there is the latest addition in the form of drones and robotic security products. These devices are then built upon by the company’s software suite, driven primarily by the already mentioned Axon Evidence platform, currently in use in 90 countries across the world, including all states in the U.S. It is a cloud-based platform that allows officials to store and manage evidence. Axon Respond and Fusus are where the company makes a real difference, allowing first responders to execute real-time operations using alerts, live video streaming, and interactive mapping. This has further improved since the emergence and implementation of AI.

It is a fusion of the above two segments that gives Axon its competitive edge. Years of perfecting its hardware and then pivoting to software has allowed it to form a business model that has high visibility into earnings thanks to recurring subscription revenue. The ecosystem, consisting of ‘Train & Prepare, Communicate & Act, and Analyze & Report’ is getting stronger thanks to regular hardware upgrades and AI integration. The CEO, Patrick W. Smith, explained the importance of the ecosystem perfectly on the Q2 earnings call when he said:

First, demand for new technology from our customers is accelerating, and it's outpacing even my most optimistic expectations. Artificial intelligence, drones and robotics, real-time operations, cameras and our newest TASER devices and virtual reality, each of those are resonating across our customer base. There's no one breakout product driving conversations. It's everything.

The company’s TASER 10 taser has become the fastest adopted taser in its history. It can hold 10 probes at a time, allowing multiple uses in a single encounter. It uses a lower output voltage and does not deliver excessive current even if more probes are discharged. These features make it safe for everyone involved in an encounter. The body cameras start recording as soon as the taser is removed from the holster, allowing for enhanced transparency. TASER 10 is just one example of how an Axon product fits into the ecosystem. The same has been observed across other offerings such as Draft One, an AI-powered transcription solution. All in all, police agencies that deploy Axon products save time, as mentioned in their Q2 2025 letter to shareholders.

Strategic Acquisitions

In pursuit of delivering a more comprehensive and holistic public safety ecosystem, AXON has been very active in expanding its product suite and tapping into new markets. The company looks to incorporate futuristic innovation and AI-enabled capabilities to offer a highly integrated solutions mix. Very recently, it announced an agreement to acquire Prepared in a deal valued between $800 million and $900 million. Prepared is a business that uses AI tools to deliver real-time situational information to emergency responders across 49 states. The company serves more than 1,000 agencies within the U.S. market. The announcement to acquire Prepared came after a series of 5 acquisitions since 2021.

The first one was the acquisition of My90 in August 2021. My90 is a highly interactive public feedback tool that delivers analytics and insights by gathering information on an anonymous basis. This data is collected from the community after they have interacted with public safety departments and agencies. The feedback is then shared with those agencies for decision-making and improvements. This acquisition was done to complement AXON’s Community Impact Team, established in 2020, with the purpose of enhancing community engagement.

In April 2022, AXON completed the acquisition of Foundry 45, a virtual reality training studio that offered enterprise-level solutions to clients, including Fortune 100 companies. After the acquisition, Foundry 45 became a part of AXON’s VR team to amplify its public safety training portfolio. In 2023, AXON made a dynamic cross-border acquisition by purchasing Sky-Hero, based in Brussels. This move strengthened AXON’s portfolio by adding unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) and unmanned ground vehicles (UGVs) capabilities. These capabilities offer real-time aerial and ground surveillance to public safety, law enforcement, and military agencies. The acquisition of Sky-Hero complemented the Axon Air powered by DroneSense program, which was launched in 2021 through a partnership with DroneSense.

AXON’s acquisition spree continued in 2024, as the business finalized two deals in the first half. First was the purchase of Fusus, which operates a Real-Time Crime Center in the Cloud (RTC3) program and uses artificial intelligence to navigate through a range of video sources for detection. Both companies had previously joined hands in 2022 in a strategic collaboration for community policing initiatives. Also in 2024, AXON entered into the airspace awareness and security segment through the acquisition of Dedrone. This move was aimed at preventing potential aerial threats and expanding on Axon’s Drone as First Responder (DFR) program called Axon Air DFR. This program was launched a few months prior to the Dedrone acquisition, and involved a partnership with Skydio X10 to integrate Axon's public safety ecosystem with Skydio's autonomous X10 drone capabilities. The acquisition of Dedrone added another layer by amalgamating Dedrone’s Beyond Visual Line of Sight (BVLOS) drone technology into the program.

In regard to some of the latest acquisitions, it is to be noted that during the second quarter of 2025, revenue from AXON’s Platform Solutions was $67 million, showcasing 86% growth compared to the same period last year. This growth was fueled by rising demand for counter-drone and VR training programs. Hence, one can take a lot of confidence from recent strategic moves and how they will lead to financial gains.

Strong Revenue Visibility

Axon’s annual recurring revenue has climbed to $1.18 billion, almost half the trailing twelve-month revenue. Over these twelve months, the total revenue has grown at 32.8% while the annual recurring revenue has grown at 39%. This strengthens the company’s business through consistent, reliable, and predictable cash flows. A part of this revenue comes from delivering additional value to existing customers. For Axon, this is nothing new. The company has kept the net revenue retention around 120% for 20 quarters now.

Going forward, the company’s revenue growth is expected to remain strong, as suggested by future contracted bookings. A 43% YoY growth in contracted bookings to $10.7 billion suggests the company is doing a great job in securing future revenue. It expects about 20% to 25% of this amount to be recognized over the next twelve months. This amounts to anywhere between $2.14 billion and $2.68 billion in revenue

Financials

Since 2019, topline grew at a CAGR of 31%, reaching $2.08 billion in 2024. During the first half of 2025, AXON registered $1.21 billion in revenues and recorded its highest quarterly revenue during the period from April to June. This also marked the 6th consecutive quarter where revenue growth was north of 30%. Strengths relating to the topline include the company’s high average revenue retention of around 120% over the last 5 years and 39% annual recurring revenue (ARR) growth. Amid these factors, management has offered strong revenue visibility for the future. With the continued expansion of the products & services portfolio, especially SaaS offerings, there lies a huge cross-selling potential for AXON.

AXON’s expansion into new segments has had a direct impact on the company’s profitability. After back-to-back years of losses in 2020 and 2021, the company turned profitable and has exhibited impressive growth in its bottom-line figures. With more high-margin offerings and a strong related demand from customers, AXON’s profitability margins have been solid. Gross margins remain within the 60%-70% range.

Although the company had an adjusted EBITDA margin of nearly 26% during the second quarter, it also recorded an operating loss of $1 million. This was caused by a $139 expense related to stock-based compensation, which is one of the risk factors discussed later in the article.

Overall, impressive results in the first half led to management’s revision of 2025 earnings guidance to a range of $2.65 to $2.73 billion. Touching the midpoint would result in 29% YoY growth. Revised guidance also indicates a full-year adjusted EBITDA between $665 million and $685 million.

AXON’s free cash flows grew from $20 million in 2020 to $330 million by the end of 2024. But they turned negative after the second quarter in 2025, given their CAPEX spending on capacity expansions and product developments. In my personal view, this trend is likely to continue in the near term as they keep exploring more opportunities for growth. But this should not discourage investors, as it will lead to long-term value creation for shareholders. I expect any announcements on negative free cash flows in the coming quarters to be overshadowed by strongly evolving business fundamentals.

At the turn of the first quarter, AXON issued senior notes with an aggregate amount of $1.75 billion to fuel future growth. Proceeds will be directed towards global expansion, developing AI capabilities, and other capital expenditures. A major concern regarding these issues is the borrowing cost. The interest rate on $1 billion 2030 notes is 6.125%, and that for $750 million 2033 notes is 6.25%. Such high financing costs will affect profitability margins going forward, but with strong revenue visibility and an impressive growth pipeline, the situation is not alarming at all.

Valuation

Judging Axon’s valuation is slightly tricky. At first glance, the company is overvalued by a long margin.

The forward P/E Non-GAAP ratio of 107.26 is 5 times the sector median, while the forward Price/Sales ratio of 20.79 is 12 times the sector median. The valuation starts to make a little more sense when we look at the 5-year average. For instance, the forward P/E is only 28% higher than the stock’s 5-year average, while the Price to Sales is only 58% higher. This suggests the stock has historically traded at higher valuations compared to the sector median, which makes the comparison unfair. Moreover, the company’s unique positioning in the public sector and its unique ecosystem allow it to leverage its products and improve earnings through new offerings relatively easily. This is one reason why the forward PEG ratio becomes important. The stock is trading 27.4% below its 5-year average, which tells me the stock is cheaper relative to its growth prospects compared to the past 5 years.

A look at peers

I will now look at how the company’s valuation compares with its peers. Axon Enterprise has built such a magnificent ecosystem that it is incredibly hard for other companies to compete. In fact, finding a direct competitor is extremely difficult. There are companies that provide similar software but do not make any hardware. There are companies that provide similar hardware but do not have as comprehensive a software suite to go with it. Axon really stands out as a monopoly, which isn’t surprising considering the global penetration it has achieved.

There are various companies that provide similar software, but even then, they serve different dimensions. For instance, Palantir Technologies Inc. (NASDAQ:PLTR) primarily serves defense and intelligence agencies, while Tyler Technologies, Inc. (NYSE:TYL) helps local governments better organize their records through its cloud offerings. Axon, on the other hand, helps law enforcement agencies. Because of the dominance in their respective fields, these companies command a higher forward P/E multiple, with Palantir at 291, Axon at 107, and Tyler Technologies at 43.85. The fact that these companies can integrate AI into their products at a fast pace helps them further separate themselves from any competition in their domain, hence the higher multiples.

There is, however, one competitor that comes quite close to what Axon does: Motorola Solutions, Inc. (NYSE:MSI). Both companies provide an ecosystem, pairing their own hardware and software to deliver solutions and services. Motorola, however, is quite broad and varied in the industries it covers. Axon, on the other hand, has a narrow focus. Since it is mostly involved with local law enforcement agencies, it has an easier time dealing with regulations than Motorola, whose predictive and facial recognition technologies are harder to implement from a political and international perspective.

Axon is valued quite highly as compared to Motorola on various metrics. Take, for instance, the difference between forward P/E ratios of 453.93 vs 35.58 or a 23.12 Price/Sales ratio for Axon compared to Motorola’s 6.74. One can attribute this difference to a simple fact: Axon is able to integrate AI into its products much more effectively, pushing quick adoption through its ecosystem, something Motorola struggles to do equally well because it serves multiple geographies and industries.

This brings me to the stock performance, because that is what helps understand the high valuation in the right context. As shown in the chart below, Axon is up over 79% for the last one year while Motorola is down 1.7%!

In my view, the high valuation is therefore justified. The post Q2 earnings dip has also brought the stock back below the 50-day moving average, and an RSI of 44 suggests the stock is by no means in overbought territory. Not at all a bad entry point for investors who want to get in on the company’s strong moat.

Risks

Having covered AXON’s promising prospects in detail, I would like to highlight some of the major risks that the investors should be aware of.

First risk concern is AXON’s high cost borrowings through $1.75 billion in senior notes issued earlier this year. This resulted in the company’s leverage ratio going up from 0.53x in 2022 to 0.63x by the end of the second quarter in 2025. Although the company looks set to benefit from its aggressive growth strategy, any near-term volatility could put it under financial burden.

Next is the risk of tariffs, which was highlighted by the management during the second-quarter earnings call. Apart from the near-term impact on AXON’s current operations, tariffs could also affect AXON’s plans for geographic expansion.

Axon’s 2024 Employee eXponential Stock Plan (XSP) and the CEO Performance Award also pose risk to the company and shareholders. From the company’s standpoint, large compensation expenses could be a pain point, while for shareholders, there are risks of dilution and long-term value creation. Axon introduced the 2024 Employee XSP following the success of XSP 1.0 approved in 2019. The initial plan resulted in value creation of $16 billion for shareholders in the following 5 years, along with a total shareholder return of more than 500%. Hence, the 2024 plan was well-received by shareholders, but there could be some negative long-term implications associated with it.

Performance targets for both the Employee XSP and the CEO Performance Award are identical and span across the next 7 years. The stock grants for the CEO and employees, who have settled for lower base salaries, will be awarded in seven tranches and are based on clearly articulated vesting criteria, shown above.

Financial impact of these plans was instant, as the stock-based compensation expense jumped by 191% in 2024. As a result of such performance-based compensation, CEO Rick Smith was the highest-paid CEO in 2024 with an annual package of $165 million. The trend carried on in 2025, as the second quarter results showed the company’s operating profits turned negative amid a $139 million stock-based compensation expense. This came after the first quarter expense of $140 million, with management remaining consistent with their previous guidance of full-year expense between $580 million and $630 million. With the expected growth of AXON, such hefty compensation packages will continue for employees and the CEO in the coming years. This anticipation is based on the company’s unrecognized compensation expenses related to Restricted Stock Units (RSUs) and Performance Stock Units (PSUs) as of March 31st, 2025. These costs were $461 million and $678 million for RSUs and PSUs, respectively.

Conclusion

AXON has a dominant market position and strong brand recognition for its legacy offerings, which will ensure recurring revenues in the years to come. Their growth strategy to diversify the existing revenue mix is what makes their business model highly attractive. The company’s ambition to integrate AI capabilities in its product suite, along with its entry into the UAV space, could prove to be a game-changer. Despite a couple of financial risks mentioned above, I am confident about the future prospects and feel comfortable with the company’s high valuation. My recommendation is Buy.

data.title: If I Could Only Buy 3 REITs, It Would Be These
data.description:
I am often get asked questions like:

What REITs would you buy if you could buy just three of them?

And I get where this is coming from.

Most investors don't care enough about real estate investment trusts, or REITs (VNQ), to build a diversified portfolio of 15-20 names. Instead, they would rather own a handful of REITs to get their real estate exposure and then diversify across other sectors of the market.

That is perfectly understandable.

You should note, however, that if I could only invest in 3 REITs, my selection would be very different than if I could build a diversified portfolio of 15-20 REITs.

The reason why is that you cannot afford to take as much risk when you have so little diversification. To give you an example: in a portfolio of 20 REITs, I could include three small-cap, heavily discounted apartment REITs that present greater upside potential but are also more heavily leveraged. But if I could only own 3 REITs, then I would rather focus on one blue-chip apartment REIT with an investment-grade-rated balance sheet because I cannot afford to be wrong. If just one of my three picks disappoints, it would significantly harm my portfolio.

With that in mind, here are the 3 REITs that I would pick today.

All 3 are what I would describe as "blue-chip" REITs in that they own Class A assets, have strong investment-grade-rated balance sheets with low leverage, and their management teams enjoy exceptional track records. Despite that, they are exceptionally discounted today and offer investors a path to market-beating returns going forward.

Each REIT also focuses on a different property sector, which affords some level of diversification, even in this smaller portfolio.

EastGroup Properties, Inc. (EGP)

EastGroup Properties (EGP) would be my pick for industrial real estate.

There are three reasons why:

Firstly, it arguably owns the best portfolio in its peer group for the long run. These are modern Class A properties, mostly located in high-barrier-to-entry last-mile locations of rapidly growing Sunbelt markets, with Florida and Texas today representing 60% of its rental income.

EGP has itself developed most of these assets, and it has kept the best one for its own portfolio, focusing on the best locations and smaller shallow-bay properties, which are much more limited in supply but in constant growing demand:

This is resulting in solid rent growth even in today's tough environment.

Beyond the portfolio, EGP has one of the lowest levels of leverage in its peer group, with a very low 10% LTV. As a result, it is one of just a few REITs that has an A- credit rating. In comparison, many of its peers use about 2x more leverage, and even that's still conservative.

It is a great advantage as it reduces the impact of higher interest rates, and also positions the REIT for faster growth going forward, as it is in a position to increase leverage to take on new development projects, which should earn it significant spreads over its cost of capital, and expand its FFO per share.

Analysts are today expecting 8% average annual growth in FFO per share through 2030:

Finally, the last reason why I would favor EGP is its management, which is truly exceptional. Just look at their multi-decade track record since they went public, outperforming even the S&P 500 (SPY):

They have massively outperformed all major market indexes by following a relatively safe strategy, and I expect this long streak of outperformance to continue going forward, especially as the AI revolution takes hold, which should greatly increase the demand for its properties. You can read why by clicking here.

But despite all these qualities, the REIT is today trading at a 50% lower valuation than just a few years ago. Its AFFO multiple has dropped from 35x to just 17x. It is also considerably lower than its 5-year average multiple of 23x:

It is also priced at a 25% discount to our estimate of its NAV, which is quite exceptional for a REIT of this quality.

I think that as interest rates are cut down further and the narrative shifts to how the AI revolution will increase the demand for industrial properties, EGP and other industrial REITs will see their valuations expand closer to historic norms.

Simply recovering to its 5-year average could unlock 30% upside potential.

But even ignoring any potential upside from multiple expansion, the REIT is well-positioned to deliver double-digit annual total returns from its 8% annual growth and its 3.5% dividend yield going forward.

The risk-to-reward is very attractive in my opinion for these reasons.

Camden Property Trust (CPT)

Camden Property Trust would be my pick for residential real estate

I would favor it over its peers for similar reasons as EGP.

Firstly, its portfolio. It owns mostly affordable Class B apartment communities in growing Sunbelt markets. Its average rent is considerably lower than that of some close peers like Mid-America Communities (MAA). Such affordable properties are always in great demand, and may even increase in demand during recessions as people downgrade from Class A to Class B to reduce their expenses.

Its rent-to-income ratios are also low, which provides great margin of safety in case of a recession, and long-term growth potential as its markets keep enjoying strong population and job growth.

CPT is a developer just like EGP, and if it has decided to retain these properties for its own portfolio, it is because they stand out for their superior quality and long-term prospects:

Secondly, it also has one of the strongest balance sheets in the sector with an A- credit rating, which should give it ample capacity to keep developing new properties going forward. Historically, the REIT has been able to develop new properties at yields 100-200 basis points higher than market cap rates, creating a lot of value for shareholders.

Finally, its management also has an exceptional track record:

On their most recent earnings call, they noted that they expect rent growth to accelerate to 4% in 2026 and 5% in 2027, which, coupled with new development projects, should result in strong growth for the REIT.

Even then, the REIT is still trading at a steep 25% discount to our estimate of its NAV. What this essentially means is that it trades at a 6.2% implied cap rate when its assets are worth closer to 5% cap in the private market.

Simply returning to NAV has the potential to deliver 30% upside, and I think that it will get there as interest rates are cut and rent growth accelerates in 2026.

Big Yellow Group Plc (BYG / OTCPK:BYLOF)

Finally, I would invest in Big Yellow Group to diversify geographically.

Big Yellow is the leading self-storage REIT in Europe, and there is a simple reason why I have owned it for close to a decade now.

Self-storage has historically been the most rewarding REIT property sector in the U.S., earning near 20% average annual total returns:

It was so rewarding because self-storage was growing in demand, and this allowed REITs to develop new properties, earning 8-10% initial yields, resulting in unusually high spreads over their cost of capital.

Moreover, REITs enjoy very significant competitive advantages in this particular sector, due to their scale and professional management. They can develop valuable brands and implement national advertising campaigns on TV, billboards, and online. Smaller landlords simply cannot compete with that.

They can also acquire properties from smaller, unsophisticated landlords and then add a lot of value by simply changing the brand, management, and then integrating the property into their booking system, benefiting from their national advertising and revenue optimization models.

But now, the American market has become very competitive, which we expect to reduce future returns.

However, the European self-storage market is still in its early days, with just 1 square foot of storage space per capita, compared to 10 in the U.S., even as the concept is rapidly growing in popularity, offering REITs lots of lucrative opportunities to develop new properties.

Big Yellow is now replicating this successful model in Europe, and it has been very successful since its inception, earning 15%+ average annual total returns for its shareholders:

It currently has 11 new projects underway, which have the potential to expand its cash flow by 15-20% over the coming two years.

In addition to that, its current portfolio has a 20% vacancy because the storage market experienced some oversupply in the post-pandemic normalization period, meaning that it has significant capacity to lease space and grow revenue in its existing portfolio.

All in all, this has the potential to result in strong growth through 2030. Even then, the company is today trading at a near-decade low valuation of 16x FFO, down from 35x at its peak:

This multiple is particularly low when you consider that its LTV is just 10%.

We also think that self-storage is another property sector that should greatly benefit from the AI revolution, as it is expected to lead to significant job market disruption, causing people to lose their jobs, which has historically been a strong demand driver, as people need to move to find new jobs, creating demand for self-storage space.

As its growth accelerates and interest rates come back down, it could surge 30% just to get back to its 5-year mean valuation, and even that may not be enough given that it has the least leverage in its history.

Ignoring the upside potential, investors can expect double-digit total returns between the 5% dividend yield and 6-8% annual growth.

Closing Note

If I were going to invest in just a few REITs, I would stick to such blue-chip REITs with low leverage, Class A assets, and proven management teams.

There are lots of small-caps in the REIT sector that are even more heavily discounted, but they are also riskier, and you probably should stay away from them unless you are willing to build a well-diversified portfolio of 15-20 names and watch it closely.

That's what we do at High Yield Landlord, and our approach has resulted in significant outperformance over the long run, but this is not for everyone.

data.title: Alibaba's AI Revolution Meets Consumer Revival
data.description:
Thesis

Alibaba (NYSE:BABA) (OTCPK:BABAF) is slowly transforming into a two-engine company focused on consumption and AI. The core China commerce platforms are regaining steam with a faster, easier, richer Taobao, while the cloud division is translating rising AI demand into a real revenue scale. International retail and logistics are stitching it all together and are broadening Alibaba’s horizon beyond China. The outcome is a more straightforward narrative than what the market is currently valuing. The company is concentrating on one consumer platform and one infrastructure platform, supported by growing engagement, a richer membership ecosystem, and a clear capital return policy. This transition from a sprawling conglomerate into a user-first, AI-powered company is why I am optimistic about Alibaba over a multi-year timeframe. Management has set forth the strategy in clear language and is executing against it.

What the Market Misses

The latest investor conversation around whether Alibaba can navigate through macro noise and competition has become stale. The primary evolving factor is that Alibaba has reorganized to fit execution to two broad pillars. On the consumer side, the company has integrated Taobao, Tmall, Ele.me, and Fliggy into a single China commerce group and put one executive in charge of all of its e commerce brands globally, which drives down internal contention and accelerates product speed. On the infra side, Alibaba Cloud is going all in on generative AI via open source models, full stack tooling, and a deeper investment plan. The common thread is a universal architecture that utilizes AI to further customer experience, merchant tools, and operations throughout the organization. This is a structural, not cyclical, change in how Alibaba competes.

China’s Commerce Is Re-Accelerating Through Pace And Purity

Taobao has raised the pace and purity of experience. Taobao Instant Commerce, rolled out back in April, delivered true on demand retail to the main app so customers can buy and receive goods in as little as an hour. They also made larger investments in customer service and membership perks that create repeat purchase cycles. The combination of those measures produced a greater take rate and customer management revenues, with double-digit gains in recent quarters.

A Second Flywheel In Commerce

Alibaba is pivoting Amap from a navigation utility to a lifestyle access point. With its new Street Stars feature, which uses an AI to rank restaurants, hotels, and spots to visit, the company has also fully supported the launch with coupons and subsidies to generate usage. And it’s working. During China’s large National Day holiday time last week, Amap had a record of more than 360 million daily active users, which signals Amap is now a viable traffic engine for local merchants and a natural cross-sell channel for Ele.me and instant commerce. This increase follows a year in which the Amap and Local Services segments began to close losses on a greater scale and better unit economics.

Cloud And AI Are A Second Growth Engine, Not Optional

Alibaba Cloud is seeing a real, quantifiable AI cycle. AI product revenue has grown at a triple-digit rate for seven consecutive quarters. In the June quarter, cloud revenue growth increased to 26 percent year on year, and management indicated that AI has emerged as a significant component. It is not merely inference APIs. The company is experiencing more appetite for compute, storage, and public cloud resources for supporting AI adoption by some internet, retail, manufacturing, and media customers.

Alibaba’s Qwen family has also become possibly the largest open source model ecosystem in the world. The Qwen3 series' advanced reasoning capabilities were entirely open source for developer adoption acceleration. The approach is pretty simple. Take capable models, easily accessible, to draw developers into Model Studio and PAI, and monetize compute, tools, and vertical apps. For the China market, where U.S. export controls limit the supply of high-end GPUs, that flywheel is still in play because most enterprise workloads would be happy with small chips or export-compliant chips with an efficient software stack.

Financials And Capital Returns

The metrics suggest a returning growth company investing in the next appendage. For the March quarter, revenues increased by 7%, and adjusted EBITA grew 36%, with cloud growing faster and Taobao customer management revenue growing 12%. For FY 2025 as a whole, revenue is up 6% and operating income is up 24% as the company leaned into technology and commerce investments as part of the User First strategy. In June, Alibaba noted that cloud revenue growth accelerated to 26% and that International Commerce was up 19% — while cash and liquid investments were approximately 586 billion yuan at quarter close.

Valuing And The Path To Re Rating

Valuation hinges on whether investors conclude that Alibaba is once again a growth compounder. The case for multiple expansion is based on three observations. First, China commerce is reaccelerating with new features like instant retail and improved member benefits improving engagement and monetization. Second, cloud is beginning a multiyear upgrade cycle as enterprises roll out generative AI across workflows, and Alibaba is already tapping into this demand at scale. Third, international commerce is nearly breakeven with positive growth, which means profit should begin to be incremental with improved unit economics. When all three engines are running at the same time, earnings power grows faster than the headline revenue, and the combination of buybacks enhances the per share effect. That is the backdrop for multiple expansions from here.

Main Risks

There is real execution risk; instant commerce is capital intensive and competes against well capitalized competitors. Subsidies could reach a prisoners’ dilemma state, if spending discipline waivers, and profitability may suffer if the company spends too aggressively. The cloud strategy rests on heavy capex and assured access to a supply of capable AI chips, in a disorganized export controls environment. Domestic chip initiatives and export compliant GPUs help, but supply friction could slow model training or increase costs. The macro risk is China is always there. Consumer sentiment, housing markets and regulation affect the pace of recovery, and the ferocity of competition. Finally, international expansion in Europe has geopolitical and compliance complexity. Each are retreatable risk but worth keeping an eye on.

Catalysts

A number of visible catalysts can move the re-rating forward. On the consumer side, the continued integration of Taobao Instant Commerce into the Double 11 and Lunar New Year cycles should result in a higher frequency of orders and customer management revenue. Amap’s rapid user growth could translate into more local advertisement and cross-selling to Ele.me, which would help shift the revenue mix toward higher margin. On the infrastructure side, another quarter of at least 20 percent cloud growth and sustained triple-digit AI product growth would validate that Alibaba’s AI strategy is scaling successfully. Management has also signaled a desire to expand AI and cloud capex above the announced three-year plan, which demonstrates confidence in the demand. Ongoing buybacks and the potential for another annual dividend add to a steady capital return cadence along with these operational milestones.

Conclusion

Alibaba has regrouped around two businesses that are compounding well together. The consumer platform is becoming faster and stickier with a membership program that now has size. The cloud platform is capitalizing on the AI wave with open models, developer-friendly tools, and a willingness to spend. For investors that can get over the old story and think about how the company is operating as of today, the risk-reward is compelling.

data.title: Hold The Horses On Texas Pacific Land
data.description:
Introduction

Texas Pacific Land (NYSE: NYSE:TPL) shocked many investors when it became one of the best performers on the stock market in 2024. Most have never heard of the company that is synonymous with the oil & gas industry in the great state of Texas. With that in mind, I want to explore the company, understand its business model and establish a potential value thesis as the company seems to have gone too far away from valuation fundamentals.

Operation Overview

TPL is relatively unique in the American Energy landscape as it leverages its landholdings in the prolific and all-mighty Permian Basin. It separates its revenue into two distinct segments: Land & Resource Management and Water Services & Operations.

Land & Resource Management is the way the company can monetize its rich inheritance by maximizing the value of the surface as well as mineral rights. Basically, TPL holds perpetual royalty interests in thousands of acres that are drilled by E&Ps, allowing TPL to benefit from Oil extraction without having to fork out the capital-intensive costs and the risks of operating wells. TPL only has to keep strong relationships with operators and encourage the development of its overall acreage. Outside of O&G royalties, the company also provides easements, commercial leases and material leases, as well as sometimes selling land. The segment has generated the majority of revenue at $480.4MM over the LTM, with O&G royalties contributing nearly $400MM.

Water Services & Operations is the other facet of TPL’s business, which recognizes the key role of water in the modern O&G industry. It provides a variety of water solutions to various operators in the basin, which is a fairly synergistic segment with L&RM. It provides solutions such as Produced Water Management, Water Sourcing and Infrastructure Development. The segment has been responsible for the company's growth while crude prices have dipped, and it was responsible for $262.5MM in overall sales for the LTM.

Current Dynamics

TPL is one of the largest landowners in the state of Texas, with around 847,000 acres, and was originally created as a business trust in 1888 to manage the property of the Texas and Pacific Railway Company. The story drastically shifted in 2016 when the company started a new strategy to maximize its asset value through active management of surface and royalty interests. This allows TPL to have great FCF generation as it did not have to buy the lands but inherited them from the original lands near the railroad.

Now the fate of the company is heavily tied to Permian production, which has seen a massive increase in production since the rise of unconventional shale, with crude production standing at around 1MMBBL/d back in 2010 to now closing in on 7MMBBL/d. This translates to a near parabolic rise in producing horizontal wells on TPL acreage to now closing in on 7,000, and Net Royalty Production at 26.8MBOE/d. Looking ahead, the Permian’s production is set to grow, albeit at a slower rate than recent years, due to both geological maturity with most Tier 1 locations already being developed, as well as the industry facing a lower price environment, which does not drive drillers to increase their drilling activity.

This is why TPL is also counting on “SLEM” to be able to capture other revenue streams outside of O&G, as it can lease for renewable forms of energy such as solar, wind and carbon capture. Though this is only 10% of the company’s consolidated revenues for FY 2024, and the segment is mainly used for O&G-derived businesses, so there is time to see if the company can develop its renewable business. Regarding the Water sales business, it has grown tremendously for a relatively “side business”, reaching 3,435MBBL/d in total produced water royalty volumes, up from 88 in 2017. That’s also thanks to the Delwara Basin having a high water-oil ratio, further increasing with the lifespan of a well. This all ties with the company’s strategy to capture all available revenue over a well lifecycle without having to commit high operating expenditures.

Valuation

By relative financial multiples, TPL is expensive with its current P/E GAAP of 46.54, over 4 times Viper Energy (VNOM) and Black Stone Minerals (BSM), as well as a pricy EV/EBITDA of 34.17, where it is important to note that the company has no debt. The company’s business model requires close to no capex and regularly highlights its FCF. At current prices, I was surprised to find a mere FCF yield of 2.2%, highlighting how the stock has risen past fundamentals. Though this premium has generally been the case for TPL due to its unique position of owning both mineral and surface rights, while having virtually no debt. However, based on my growth assumptions for the company with the O&G royalties business flattening in the near future, and with a relatively bullish stance, my DCF modelling never gave me an implied share price that could justify this current price with these conditions, with the highest being at around $530.

Regarding growth, TPL has been able to grow its revenue at 7.71% CAGR over the past 3 years and its net income by 6.74% CAGR, while the industry has been grappling with falling crude prices since the peak back in 2022. Looking ahead, the Permian is set to see slower growth as the company is inextricably linked to the region’s long-term output and valuation multiples are linked to the parabolic rise in volume, which should not be the case in the future. Water Services & Operations is the most immediate growth driver, which should continue in the mid-term as TPL is actively deploying capital to build infrastructure crucial for non-conventional shale players, increasing earnings in a flat /lower commodity price dynamic. SLEM should be the long-term growth story for the company, though we will have to wait and see if it can create a strong relationship with renewable energy providers. Thus, with the kind of outlook for both crude and the Permian, I expect O&G royalties to dip in the remainder of the year, further depreciating share prices while the water business continues its growth. In the medium term, I expect O&G royalties to stay flat while the company continues to scale its other business, thus we could see a lower rate of return of capital to shareholders.

Profitability is a bright star for TPL as the company has close to no operating costs, as it simply leases its land for the bulk of its revenue, though it has expanded its water services, which have reduced operating margins over time from 86.9% in 2018 to now 73.8%. The company has been able to drastically improve its FCF margin over time to an astonishing level, and one of the highest on the market at 65.2% thanks to its asset-light model. And the great part of the TPL‘s business model would be that, despite lower realized prices, hurting O&G royalties, the company was able to boost all other aspects of its business, acting as a buffer. With my vision of the future for TPL, I believe that we will continue to see operating margins dip as the company becomes more and more reliant on capital measures in its water business to pick up the pace of the O&G royalty side, making its bottom line more in line with the rest of its peers, thus depreciating the valuation metrics.

TPL also drastically increased its capital return to shareholders since its IPO, with around $70MM back in 2018 to now over $376M in 2024. The dividend is fairly low at 0.69% though it has increased by a massive 13.94% CAGR over the past 5 years. The company also buys back its shares at opportune times when the market does not recognize the full value of the company and it is heavily focusing on FCF for shareholders, thus further boosting its capital return policy.

Conclusion

I believe that TPL is a great company with a rather unique business model. It is a company to buy at opportune times but the time is not right now in my opinion. It’s highly touted FCF metric only gives shareholders a 2.2% FCF yield at current prices, while its capital return policies are simply too small at current prices as well. The company is well-footed in the energy center of America, but with the recent headwinds, I believe that the stock price should be lower, which should then offer an opportunity for investors. I rate Texas Pacific Land as a Hold.

data.title: The Mint IPO: Spectacular Gains Obscure A Precarious Future
data.description:
Since its initial public offering (IPO), Mint Incorporation Limited’s (NASDAQ:MIMI) share price has nearly doubled, rising by 95%, compared to around 15% for both the Shares Russell 3000 ETF (IWV) and the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY). This spectacular stock performance is in stark contrast with the firm’s declining fundamentals. Added to that, there are new risks from the firm’s pivot to robotics that make the company more unattractive. With sky high valuations, there is enormous downside to the firm’s share price.

Business Model

Although it was incorporated in the British Virgin Islands on October 18, 2023, Mint’s operations are conducted in the Hong Kong Special Administration Region (SAR). Mint itself is a holding company, whose two subsidiaries, CKL Holding Ltd. and Aspiration Group Ltd., are also incorporated in the British Virgin Islands. However, Mint and these subsidiaries do not engage in any business. The company’s operating subsidiary, Matter Interiors Ltd., is organised under the laws of and operates in the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region (SAR), and was formed on November 16, 2018.

As Mint explains in its 20-F, through Matter Interiors Ltd., the company engages in interior design and is a fit-out works, “providing integrated and industry-specific interior design and fit-out works for commercial properties.” This work is done in office spaces across various industries, and in retail stores, and aims to reflect their clients' corporate values and reflect their brands. The company’s clients include many globally known offices, retail stores, food and beverage outlet chains, and locations owned by an important charity. The company also serves luxury residential properties in the SAR. These projects fall into two categories:

“(i) design services, in which we develop and create tailor-made interior design proposals; and (ii) design and fit out services, in which we undertake the overall project management, coordination and quality control, and supervise fit out works carried out by our subcontractors, complemented by other services such as repair and maintenance works and procurement of furniture and fit out materials, etc.”

In fulfilling its mission, the company offers design services and design and fit-out services. Design services involve the company’s designers learning about the particular needs of their customers, and the industries they operate in, and coming up with various design ideas that reflect what they have learned. These are edited into two-dimensional design proposals created through Adobe or AutoCAD that are presented to the customers. After an iterative process, a final three-dimensional design proposal is created using computer-aided design (CAD) systems. Design and fit-out services involve the just-discussed design services as well as fit-out services. This category made up the bulk of the firm’s projects in fiscal years (FY) 2023 and 2024. Fit-out services involve project managers monitoring the progress of design projects, supervising subcontractors, making on-site visits, and conducting quality inspections. Design and fit out services span the process from design services to rectifying defects in the defects liability period six to twelve months after the certificate of completion has been handed over.

Business tends to come from private companies and individuals, with recurring customers and referrals from current or previous customers providing the majority of business. When a project is initiated, customers pay a deposit of between 20% and 40% to the company. The initial phase generally lasts a month or less.

Growth Plan Is a Major Pivot

As part of its strategic growth plan, on Sept. 15, the company announced the launch of another operating subsidiary, Axonex Intelligence Limited, organised under the laws of Hong Kong. Touted as an "expansion into the technology realm with the fast-growing smart facility management sector”, Axonex is a way for Mint to “harness robotics, IoT, and AI technologies to enhance the way properties are managed and experienced.” It is not clear if Axonex has begun operations, as the language of the announcement shifts from the present to the future tense:

“Axonex provides total solutions for smart facility management, integrating advanced technologies to improve efficiency, safety, and user experience across different property types. Its key capabilities include:

  • Robotic solutions to automate routine facility management tasks, improve efficiency, and reduce manpower needs.

  • Object identification and human posture recognition for enhanced security and operational monitoring.

  • Digital twin solutions enabling real-time visualization and management of facilities.

  • AI-powered analytics to optimize maintenance, space utilization, and resource allocation.

Axonex is set to serve a diverse range of clients, including shopping malls, government premises, residential complexes, and warehouses. By leveraging its design background and new technological focus, Axonex aims to provide a holistic approach to property and facility management.”

Management believes that collaborations with tech partners such as Aishang Mobility Limited, and Sharp Peak Consulting Limited, will allow it to deliver meaningful integration of hardware and software. Theoretically, this should improve efficiencies, reduce costs, and allow boosted growth. However, it is notable that, prior to this announcement, there was no mention of robots, artificial intelligence, or even innovation, in the 20-F. The rudeness of the move, and the seeming lack of expertise in the area, despite management’s talk of a pedigree of innovation, make this pivot suspect. Execution risk will be high, although the tech partnerships will mitigate that to some extent, and caution is warranted because in the “build or buy” debate in tech, there is a view that it is cheaper for firms to simply use third-party technology rather than try to build it themselves. Margins may decline rather than increase, as a result of investments to build out this new subsidiary. It is not surprising that the share price has fallen from $10.75 at the close of trading on 15 Sept. to $7.78 at the time of writing.

Voluntary Lock-Ups Are A Show Of Confidence

The share price decline in the wake of the Axonex announcement seems to have triggered a show of confidence on the part of management and key shareholders. The recent announcement of voluntary lock-up agreements between the company and certain shareholders, including Mint’s chairman, Cheong Shing Ku, and its chief executive officer, Hoi Hung Chan, is a deliberate signal to the market that management and key shareholders believe in the direction of the company. The agreements cover some 26.7% of Mint’s class A shares, and 100% of its class B shares, with the lock-up period beginning on Sept. 30, 2025, and ending on March 31, 2026. Chan explained that

“These voluntary lock-up agreements demonstrate our confidence in Mint’s vision and the execution of our growth strategy. As Mint is extending its expertise to harness robotics, IoT, and AI technologies to enhance the way properties are managed and experienced, we believe this commitment will further reinforce long-term shareholder confidence and support sustainable value creation.”

This will have the unintended consequence of increasing the volatility of the share price: with fewer shares trading, the impact of sales or purchases will be increased. That is the short-term impact. That said, this show of confidence certainly shows that key people believe in this project.

Unimpressive Recent Financial Performance Looms Large

A key reason for this strategic pivot may be that the company’s overall financial performance has been rather unimpressive. Revenue has compounded by 15.54% a year since FY2022. However, revenue fell from $4.38 million in FY2024 to $3.27 million in FY2025, a decline of 25%. That is an alarming decline. The company’s net income declined from $0.26 million in FY2022 to -$1.46 million in FY2025. The loss in FY2025 was driven by a combination of a collapse in revenue and a surge in general and administrative expenses, from $669,825 in FY2024 to $2.3 million in FY2025. This led to a fall in net income from $784,618 in FY2024 to -$1.46 million in FY2025. In the 20-F, you can see that staff and benefit expenses were the biggest driver of growth in general and administrative expenses, followed by office expenses. Overall, management explained that the increase in general and administrative expenses was due to, “the increase in IPO bonus, listing expense, and office expense resulting from our initial public offering.”

Although one-time IPO related expenses will go down, this will be balanced against increases related to the costs of being a publicly traded company.

Sky High Valuation

At the current price, Mint has a price/sales ratio of 51.03. This is in stark comparison with Seeking Alpha’s estimate of the sector median of 1.68, and Prof. Aswath Damodaran’s sector median estimate of 1.26. If we consider that the company is coming off the back of declining revenue, and faces execution risks from a novel pivot into robotics, there is a rational basis for the view that the company’s valuation will decline. If that is the case, it should fall toward sector medians. If that happens, the company faces steep downsides.
